Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study in the evenings after my extracurriculars, but most nights I'd just feel this heaviness and end up listening to lecture recordings while doing other things. I spent a lot of hours technically studying, but I wasn't really present for a lot of it. Sometimes I'd have these bursts where I'd try to catch up on everything at once, but it never felt sustainable.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in and listening to materials at the same times. But then anxiety would hit and I'd avoid everything for days, only to cram when deadlines got close. The stress of having 14 courses made it impossible to have a real schedule—I was always reacting to whatever was most urgent rather than planning ahead.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd realize I hadn't checked anything in days and force myself to open the LMS. Sometimes a notification about a deadline would trigger it. Occasionally a friend from my peer support group would mention something from class and I'd feel embarrassed that I didn't know about it, so I'd log in to catch up.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in daily for maybe a week, but it never stuck. The LMS felt like this reminder of everything I was falling behind on, so logging in became something I dreaded rather than a habit. I wish I could say it became routine, but my engagement was always inconsistent.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I could download them and listen while walking to my activities or lying in bed when I felt too drained to sit at a desk. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful too—seeing other students ask questions made me feel less alone in my confusion.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and written resources. My brain just doesn't absorb information that way, and with my anxiety, staring at long texts made me feel overwhelmed. I also ignored a lot of the optional quizzes and practice activities. They felt like extra weight when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to passing the course or if it was in a format I could actually absorb. Listening to a clear explanation that connected concepts—that felt valuable. But busy work or things that seemed disconnected from what would be on exams? I couldn't justify the mental energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had deadlines that forced me to act, even if I was anxious about them. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing it. The required work gave me structure; the optional stuff felt like another way to fail.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them on repeat, sometimes while trying to fall asleep. I also had a study group through my peer support network where we'd meet in person and talk through concepts. None of that showed up in the system, but it's how I actually learned.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be lying in bed with my earbuds in, replaying lecture recordings, trying to understand concepts. Or I'd be at a coffee shop with friends from my peer group, working through problems together on paper. The LMS would show I downloaded something once, but not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q11: Constantly. I'd download the audio files or save lecture slides once, then never open them in the LMS again. I'd study from those files for weeks. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login frequency suggested. I just preferred having everything offline where I could access it without the anxiety of seeing all my incomplete tasks.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked and I felt this constant sadness that made everything feel pointless. I'd look at the LMS and see all these courses and incomplete tasks and just shut down. The peer support helped me push through eventually, but there were weeks where I could barely bring myself to engage with anything academic.
Q13: Absolutely. With 14 courses, I was constantly overwhelmed. The fatigue from juggling everything, plus my extracurriculars, meant I had to triage constantly. Some courses got neglected while I focused on others. When my anxiety was high, even opening the LMS felt like too much, so I'd avoid it entirely and fall further behind.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind was somewhere else entirely—worrying about other assignments, feeling sad, just zoning out. The watch time counted, but I wasn't really there. Sometimes I'd have to replay the same lecture three or four times before anything actually stuck.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the ones that required writing or felt overwhelming in scope. My anxiety made starting things incredibly hard—I'd know something was important but feel paralyzed. The bigger and more important it seemed, the more I'd avoid it. It's a terrible cycle that I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd ignore things until the pressure became unbearable, then scramble at the last minute. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. It's not healthy, but without that external pressure, my anxiety and sadness made it too easy to drift.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. Positive feedback from peers or instructors gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for a while. My resilience usually brought me back eventually, but it took time.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many components spread across different modules. I needed clearer guidance on what was essential versus optional. When things were well-organized with clear audio explanations, I could follow along. But when the structure was confusing, my anxiety made it hard to even figure out where to start.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement easier for me—that matched how I learn. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made engagement harder. Also, when discussion posts were required but felt performative rather than genuine, I'd do the minimum. Authentic peer interaction, like in my support group, was much more engaging.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd browse around, watch videos, check different sections—generating activity—but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The anxiety of producing something that would be judged was way harder than passively consuming content. High activity doesn't mean someone feels capable of completing things.
Q21: That also resonates with me. My exam score was okay and I passed, but my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning through downloaded materials and peer discussions that weren't tracked. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was just learning in ways the system couldn't see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent. But they can't show understanding, emotional state, or offline engagement. They miss the student listening to a downloaded lecture at 2 AM while battling anxiety. They miss the peer conversations that actually clarified concepts. They capture presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when my mind is present and I'm connecting ideas, asking questions, wanting to know more. For me, real engagement often happened outside the LMS—in conversations, in quiet moments of reflection. It's internal, not just behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my mental health affected my engagement—the days I couldn't get out of bed, the anxiety that made logging in feel impossible. It wouldn't show the peer support sessions that kept me going, or the hours I spent with downloaded materials. It wouldn't show that despite everything, I was trying really hard in ways that just weren't visible.
